questions.prompt: Which best describes the final assessment's shape?
choices: Fifteen scored scenarios spanning Courses 2 through 8, each multi-decision, designed to test calibration through realistic ticket choices.

choices.label: A timed multiple-choice test on portal menu paths.
choices.rationale: The lesson explicitly states menu paths are not tested (they change).

choices.label: A spoken interview with a senior tech.
choices.rationale: Wrong format. The senior conversation is the next stage after passing.

choices.label: A practical exercise on a live Huntress instance.
choices.rationale: The assessment is scenario-based in the LMS, not live-platform work.

questions.explanation: Fifteen scenarios, multi-decision branching, calibration over memorisation.

questions.prompt: Which two scenario categories are weighted for review even when the overall score is above 80%?

choices.label: EDR (Course 4) and SIEM (Course 7).
choices.rationale: EDR is bread-and-butter scope. SIEM may be skipped entirely if the MSP does not run it.

choices.label: Onboarding (Course 2) and communication (Course 8).
choices.rationale: Both are tested but neither is the flagged category.

choices.label: Only the judgement scenarios (Course 9).
choices.rationale: Course 9 scenarios appear but are not the named weighted categories.

questions.explanation: Critical and ITDR are the highest-blast-radius categories. Getting them wrong suggests the calibration is not yet right for live work in those areas.

questions.prompt: Which of these is NOT directly tested by the assessment?

choices.label: Memorisation of portal menu paths.
choices.rationale: Explicitly out of scope (they change).

choices.label: Knowledge of specific runbooks.
choices.rationale: Explicitly out of scope (these are your MSP's).

choices.label: Familiarity with specific customers.
choices.rationale: Explicitly out of scope (you do not have any yet).

choices: All three are out of scope. The assessment tests calibration of judgement instead.
questions.explanation: The assessment tests calibration via scenarios, not memorisation, runbook recall, or customer knowledge.

questions.prompt: Order the steps of the standard MSP sign-off process from passing the assessment to unsupervised live work.
items: Work live tickets under direct senior review.
Cleared for unsupervised work at the boundary the course describes.
questions.explanation: Pass, review, shadow, supervised, unsupervised. The assessment-pass triggers step 2; the senior's sign-off gates each subsequent step.

questions.prompt: Assessment scenario: a customer's IT manager pushes for a host release the SOC has not approved. Options include release per customer request, escalate to senior, approve a modified remediation, and close as a customer-managed event. Which calibration test should you load?
choices: The two-question test: is this reversible? Do I understand why this is the right action? If either is no, the right option is usually escalate. This is also a clean category-6 trip.

choices.label: The 'speed is the goal' test. Pick the fastest option.
choices.rationale: Inverted lesson. Speed-is-the-goal is a misconception, not a test.

choices.label: Whatever the customer wants. The assessment is about customer service.
choices.rationale: Customer preference does not override the escalation categories.

choices.label: The severity band. If it is High, contain; if Low, close.
choices.rationale: Severity drives tempo, not the customer-versus-SOC calibration.

questions.explanation: The two-question test loads throughout the assessment. Customer-versus-SOC tension is category 6: the senior owns it.

questions.prompt: "If I memorise every lesson, I'll pass." What is the right correction?
choices: Memorisation helps; calibration matters more. The scenarios test how the lessons apply to new situations, not whether you remember the lesson text.

choices.label: Memorisation is sufficient; the assessment is mostly recall.
choices.rationale: Inverts the lesson's framing.

choices.label: Memorisation is irrelevant; only practice on live tickets matters.
choices.rationale: Over-corrects. Memorisation helps; it just is not enough.

choices.label: Memorise the runbooks specifically; the lessons are not tested.
choices.rationale: Runbooks are out of scope; lessons are exactly what is tested.

questions.explanation: Passing depends on the reflex landing under scenario pressure, not on recall under quiz pressure.

questions.prompt: "Passing means I'm ready for everything Huntress can throw at me." What is wrong with this?
choices: Passing means the LMS recommends you are ready for the boundary the course describes. Your MSP's sign-off and ongoing experience extend the boundary over time; passing does not grant scope beyond the course.

choices.label: Passing is meaningless. Only the senior's sign-off matters.
choices.rationale: Over-corrects. The pass is meaningful; it is not the only gate.

choices.label: Passing means you can take any action on any incident.
choices.rationale: The mandatory-escalation categories still apply.

choices.label: Passing means you can override SOC recommendations going forward.
choices.rationale: The cardinal mistake still applies regardless of assessment score.

questions.explanation: The course teaches a bounded scope. Passing recommends readiness within that scope, not beyond it.

questions.prompt: Which set of lessons does 9.5 specifically recommend re-reading before the assessment?
choices: 1.2 (managed-SOC keystone), 1.8 (recommendation vs context), 4.5 (when to isolate), 5.1 (Critical markers), 6.1 (identity blast-radius), 9.1 (mandatory escalation), 9.4 (common traps).

choices.label: Every lesson in Courses 0 through 9, in order.
choices.rationale: Wrong scope. The lesson specifies pattern lessons, not the whole course.

choices.label: Only the Course 9 lessons (9.1 through 9.5).
choices.rationale: Too narrow. The pattern lessons span Courses 1, 4, 5, 6, and 9.

choices.label: The SIEM lessons (Course 7) because they are the most technical.
choices.rationale: SIEM may be skipped entirely if your MSP does not run it.

questions.explanation: Pattern lessons whose judgement shows up most across scenarios. These are the load-bearing lessons.

questions.prompt: The lesson closes with: "The most reliable instinct, when in doubt, is the one this course has been installing." What is that instinct?

choices.label: Whichever option mentions Huntress support is probably correct.
choices.rationale: Misuses support and inverts the course's discipline.

choices: Trust the SOC's Recommendation, act on the action zone, surface context to the right body, escalate at the categories, and do not click-and-see.

choices.label: The longest option is usually the right one.
choices.rationale: Length is not a correctness signal.

choices.label: Always pick 'escalate' regardless of context.
choices.rationale: Over-escalates. Routine work proceeds; the categories are the trip points.

questions.explanation: The course's reflexes compressed into the in-doubt heuristic. When in doubt, trust the SOC, act on the action zone, surface context, and do not click-and-see.

questions.prompt: Assessment scenario: unfamiliar detection with a clean Recommendation, but the Evidence surfaces additional signals the Recommendation does not mention. What is the right move?

choices.label: Approve the remediation and also act on the extra signals.
choices.rationale: Extending the action set on context the Recommendation does not cover is the cardinal mistake.

choices: Approve the remediation per the Recommendation. Raise the extra signals as a clarifying reply to the SOC.

choices.label: Close as benign because the extra signals look suspicious but the Recommendation only mentions autoruns.
choices.rationale: Closing without acting overrides the SOC's Recommendation. The extra Evidence is a reason to clarify, not close.

choices.label: Escalate the entire incident to the senior and do not act on any of it.
choices.rationale: Over-escalates. The Recommendation's action zone is in scope; the extra signals are the clarifying reply.

questions.explanation: Execute the Recommendation's action zone. Surface additional Evidence as a factual clarifying reply. The analyst weighs the extra signals.
